about: The propaganda playbook: named, recognizable manipulation techniques. Prebunking (inoculation theory: McGuire 1961; Roozenbeek & van der Linden 2019–22) shows that teaching people the SHAPE of a manipulation before they meet it in the wild measurably reduces its effect. Each technique names the shape, the tell, and the counter.

techniques.name: Nation retrojection
techniques.definition: Projecting a modern nation-state onto a past that predates it, to claim (or sever) heritage.
techniques.tell: A modern country's name or flag attached to people/objects older than the country: 'ancient Pakistani grammarian', 'Pakistan: a 9,000-year-old civilisation'.
techniques.counter: Name the founding date next to the claimed date. 'Pakistan: founded 1947, name coined 1933. Pāṇini: ~5th century BCE. The gap is 2,400 years.' Then supply what was severed: what tradition the person/object actually belonged to.
techniques.example: "Sanskrit was codified by the ancient Pakistani grammarian Panini"

techniques.name: Founding-date fallacy
techniques.definition: Treating an evolved, unfounded tradition as if it began on a datable day, so something can be declared 'older than' it.
techniques.tell: 'X is N years older than Hinduism' — a subtraction that requires a start date no scholar can give.
techniques.counter: 'Older than Hinduism' is like 'older than the English language' — evolved things don't have birthdays. Then note the irony where present: the artifact cited is often one continuity scholars point INTO the tradition.
techniques.example: "This seal is 2,400 years older than Hinduism!"

techniques.name: Word–thing fallacy
techniques.definition: Arguing the thing is recent (or fake) because the WORD for it is recent.
techniques.tell: 'The word Hindu appears in no scripture' / 'Hinduism was invented in 1830' offered as proof the religion itself is a construction.
techniques.counter: Names are newer than things: 'Byzantine Empire' was coined in 1557. Show the referent's continuity (texts, ritual grammar, pilgrimage networks) and the insiders' own umbrella terms (sanātana dharma, vaidika dharma) — plus documented pre-colonial 'Hindu' self-identity (Lorenzen 1999).
techniques.example: "There was no Hinduism before the British — the word didn't exist."

techniques.name: Severed origin
techniques.definition: Detaching a practice, discovery, or figure from the tradition it demonstrably belongs to — the appropriation move.
techniques.tell: 'X has nothing to do with Hinduism' / origin reassigned wholesale to another culture ('Arabic numerals', 'yoga is just exercise').
techniques.counter: Produce the charter documents: the texts that constitute the practice, with dates. For numerals: the Arabs' own name for them was 'Indian reckoning'. For yoga: Kaṭha Upaniṣad, Gītā 6, Yogasūtras. Concede honestly what IS shared or modern — that concession is what makes the core stick.
techniques.example: "Yoga isn't Hindu — it's a universal wellness practice."

techniques.name: Term inflation
techniques.definition: Upgrading an entity with a grander modern label than the evidence supports.
techniques.tell: 'University', 'empire', 'metric system', 'first in the world' where the sources show something real but different.
techniques.counter: Give the accurate term and show it's impressive enough: 'Taxila was among the ancient world's most famous hubs of higher learning' needs no inflation — and inflating it cheapens Nālandā's real institutional claim.

techniques.name: Context stripping
techniques.definition: A real quote presented without the genre, adjacent text, or contested authority that changes its meaning.
techniques.tell: A single translated verse screenshot + 'this is what Hinduism teaches'. No genre, no counter-verses, no note on whether the text carries the claimed authority.
techniques.counter: Restore all three contexts: genre (jurists' treatises that disagree with each other), adjacency (the verses beside it), and authority (who actually treats this text as binding — often: the British legal system, more than Hindu communities).

techniques.name: Literalized idiom
techniques.definition: A polysemous or idiomatic expression frozen at its most sensational literal reading.
techniques.tell: A big round number or lurid image doing rhetorical work: '330 million gods'.
techniques.counter: Show the word's semantic range and the source text's own resolution — Bṛhadāraṇyaka 3.9.1 walks the count from 3,306 down to 33 down to one.

techniques.name: Fabricated endorsement
techniques.definition: A prestige institution or genius invented as the claim's sponsor.
techniques.tell: 'NASA declared...', 'Einstein said...' + superlative + no link. The citation is always missing or circular.
techniques.counter: One question: 'link the declaration.' Then supply the real kernel if one exists (a 1985 AI Magazine paper by one researcher) — the honest version is usually still interesting.
techniques.example: "NASA says Sanskrit is the best language for computers."

techniques.name: Kernel inflation
techniques.definition: A real but modest fact inflated into a grand institutional claim.
techniques.tell: The chain from 'a researcher wrote a paper' to 'NASA is building Sanskrit computers' — each retelling adds a rank.
techniques.counter: Locate the kernel, credit it precisely, and show the inflation steps. Precision is the rebuttal.

techniques.name: Mythology as engineering
techniques.definition: Reading epic imagery as technical documentation — flying chariots as aircraft, divine weapons as nuclear physics.
techniques.tell: 'Ancient India had aircraft/nuclear weapons/plastic surgery' sourced to epic narrative or to backdated modern texts.
techniques.counter: Check the text's provenance (the Vaimānika Śāstra is from 1900–1922) and the physics (IISc 1974: none of it flies). Then point at the REAL record — Piṅgala, zero, Kerala series, Suśruta — which needs no inflation and is corroded by it.
techniques.example: "The Vaimanika Shastra contains ancient aircraft blueprints."

techniques.name: Prestige borrowing
techniques.definition: Using the credibility of a tradition's genuine achievements to launder fabricated ones — or using its fabrications to dismiss the genuine ones.
techniques.tell: Real and fake achievements bundled in one list; or a debunked overclaim used to wave away the documented record ('they lie about vimanas, so the zero claim is also fake').
techniques.counter: Unbundle. Verdict each claim separately. This is exactly why tathya's category 9 exists: correcting our own side's inflations is what keeps the documented record unassailable.
techniques.example: Listicle mixing zero (real) with vimāna aircraft (fake).

techniques.name: Exception as norm
techniques.definition: A real but bounded practice presented as the tradition's universal, timeless command.
techniques.tell: 'Hinduism commands X' where the record shows regional/temporal boundedness and internal opposition.
techniques.counter: Concede the reality fully (satī happened; the numbers are documented), then restore the boundaries and the internal opposition — Ṛgveda 10.18.8 sends the widow back to the living; jurists condemned it; a Hindu reformer led the abolition.

techniques.name: Colonial category laundering
techniques.definition: A schema manufactured or hardened by colonial administration circulating as timeless indigenous fact.
techniques.tell: Census-shaped 'facts': one pan-India caste ladder, one law book, 'unchanged for 3,000 years'.
techniques.counter: Date the schema: 1871–1901 censuses, 1794 Anglo-Hindu law. Then state honestly what predates it (hierarchy and untouchability are old and indigenous) — the both-directions correction is the credible one.
techniques.example: "The caste system: the same four boxes since the Vedas."

techniques.name: Slur / dehumanization
techniques.definition: Not an argument at all: disgust imagery and slur lexicons attached to a people.
techniques.tell: 'Pajeet', 'cow piss', disgust memes replying to anything Indian or Hindu. There is no claim to debunk.
techniques.counter: Don't debate a slur. Document it (screenshot, hash, report), cite the prevalence research (NCRI 2022), and hold the line: criticism of ideas is legitimate; dehumanizing people is not — in both enforcement directions.
techniques.example: Slur-swarm replies under any Hinduism post.

techniques.name: False certainty
techniques.definition: An open scholarly question asserted as settled — in either direction.
techniques.tell: 'Debunked', 'proven', 'settled science' attached to questions the field itself calls open (Indus–Vedic continuity, Aryan migration details, Indus script).
techniques.counter: State the actual score: what each side's best evidence is, with citations, and what remains open. Protecting the genuinely open question (category 12) is part of busting propaganda about it.
techniques.example: "Aryan Migration is a debunked colonial hoax" / "Invasion destroyed the Indus cities — settled."
